frontmatter.title: Appendix F: Which Method When
frontmatter.subtitle: MATH 4210 -- a decision guide from data and question to procedure

nameParsed.literal: Anjana Yatawara, Department of Mathematics, CSU Bakersfield

authors.name: Anjana Yatawara, Department of Mathematics, CSU Bakersfield

content.name: Creative Commons Attribution Share Alike 4.0 International

children.value:
This appendix is a map. You bring a dataset and a question; it points you to the
method this book teaches for that situation, and to the section where the method
lives. It does not re-teach anything. Every arrow ends at an

you can
follow back into the chapter that explains the details, states the assumptions,
and shows the R and Python code.
Read it as a companion to the workflow spine of

(what is
the question and what are the data),

(interpret,
predict, decide, communicate). This guide mostly helps at ASK and FIT, points you
to the right CHECK tools, and tells you what to do when a check fails.
Work through it in three questions, in order:
What kind of thing is your response variable?
What are you actually trying to find out?
Which assumptions hold, and what do you do when one breaks?
F.1 Start with the response: the master fork

children.label: F.1 Start with the response: the master fork

children.value: The first fork is the type of the response variable

children.html: YYY

children.value:
, the thing you want to
explain or predict. Almost everything else follows from it. Find your row.

children.html: YYY

children.value: work hours, savings rate, body fat percent
7.1 The model and least squares in matrix form
13.1 Why a straight line fails, and what to use instead
14.1 Why counts break the linear model
15.5 Forecasting honestly: test on the future

In words: continuous responses are the home ground of ordinary least squares
(most of this book); binary and count responses need the generalized linear model
of
14.6 One family: the generalized linear model

, which contains linear and logistic and Poisson regression as three
members of one family; time order is not a response type so much as a warning that
the independence assumption is in danger, handled in Chapter 15.

A common wrong turn: fitting ordinary linear regression to a 0/1 response because
the software runs without complaint.
13.1 Why a straight line fails, and what to use instead

shows why that gives
fitted “probabilities” below 0 and above 1, and what to do instead.
F.2 Then ask what you want to find out

children.label: F.2 Then ask what you want to find out

children.value:
Once the response type has chosen a method family, your question chooses what you
do inside it. The table reads within the continuous-response world first, since
that is where the book spends most of its time; the last rows cross into the other
families.
How are two continuous variables related? Fit a line.
How strong and trustworthy is that slope?

16.1 Drawing a causal story: path diagrams

Two questions this book is careful about, because software will answer them even
when it should not: “keep or drop these predictors” (

warns that
choosing predictors and then testing them on the same data inflates your
confidence), and “does X cause Y” (
16.4 What path analysis cannot claim about causation

and the correlation-is-not-
causation framing of

set the limits of what any regression can claim
from observational data).

This is the largest branch, so it gets its own walk-through. Follow the questions
top to bottom.

With a single continuous predictor, fit the simple
linear model

children.html: Yi=β0+β1Xi+εiY_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_i + \varepsilon_iYi​=β0​+β1​Xi​+εi​

children.value:
), do
inference on the slope with

, and if you only want a
direction-free measure of association use correlation (

children.html: rrr

children.value:
and the slope carry the same sign and are two views of one fit). With two
or more predictors, move to the matrix-form model

children.html: Y=Xβ+ε\mathbf{Y} = \mathbf{X}\beta + \varepsilonY=Xβ+ε

Do you need to predict, and how sure must you be?

For the average response at
a given

children.html: XXX

children.value:
, use a confidence interval for the mean; for a single new observation,
use the wider prediction interval (
3.5 Prediction interval for a new observation

). The gap between the
two is one of the most-missed distinctions in the course.
F.4 When an assumption breaks: detect, then remedy

children.label: F.4 When an assumption breaks: detect, then remedy

children.value:
Ordinary least squares earns its good properties (unbiased, minimum variance among
linear estimators) from the Gauss-Markov conditions of

: the
mean is linear in the predictors, the errors have constant variance, and they are
uncorrelated. Normality is added only for exact

children.html: ttt
FFF

children.value:
inference. Chapter 9
is the toolbox for finding out which of these fails; Chapter 10 is the toolbox for
fixing it. This table pairs the two.

Two habits keep you out of trouble. First, always CHECK before you USE: a slope
with a tiny

shows how a log turns a slope into a
percent-change statement, which is a feature, not a nuisance.

There is also the honest limit case. When the sample is small and you cannot trust
the normal-theory

children.html: ttt
FFF

children.value:
at all, do not pretend. The permutation test and the
bootstrap (
5.2 The permutation test for the slope

) ask for far less and give you
a

children.html: ppp

children.value: 5.1 When normal-theory inference is on thin ice

is the section that tells
you when the classical machinery is standing on thin ice.

Here is the guide used once, start to finish, on a real thread from the book: the
Galapagos species data, where each island records the number of plant species it
holds along with its area, elevation, and distance from other islands.

The response is a count of species: 0, 1,
2, and up, never negative, never fractional. The master fork of Section F.1 sends
counts to the Poisson / GLM family (
14.1 Why counts break the linear model
But the book tried linear first, on purpose.

In Chapter 9 the same data is fit
with ordinary least squares, and the diagnostics of Section F.4 fire loudly:
Isabela, a huge island, sits at an extreme leverage value, and the residual
variance grows with the fitted count, breaking constant variance. That is
the “variance grows with the mean” and “high-leverage points” rows of the remedy
table.

The constant-variance failure first suggests a
transformation (
10.3 Box-Cox: letting the data choose the power
10.5 When a transformation is not enough: the Galapagos counts

shows that a log helps
but does not truly fix a count response: islands with zero or few species strain
any transformation of

children.html: YYY

children.value:
The clean resolution is the one the response type
pointed to all along: model the counts with Poisson regression and a log link
(
14.1 Why counts break the linear model

), where the variance is allowed to grow with the mean by
design.

adds the
one more check you owe a count model: whether the spread is even larger than
Poisson allows, in which case you widen to quasi-Poisson.

The lesson of the walk-through is the shape of the guide itself. The response type
named the destination at Section F.1; the questions and the diagnostics explained
why the detour through linear regression was worth taking and where it had to stop.
When you just need the fast answer, this is the whole appendix in one view.

If two rows seem to fit, work the questions of Sections F.1 to F.3 in order. The
response type wins ties: it decides the family, and the family decides the rest.
